data.title: Muthoot Finance to reissue multiple tenor bonds, bankers say
data.description:
India's Muthoot Finance plans to raise 18 billion rupees ($210.2 million), including a greenshoe option of 15 billion rupees, through the reissue of 8.50% May 2028 and 8.20% April 2030 bonds, three bankers said on Friday.

The non-banking finance company will invite bids from bankers and investors on Tuesday, they said.

Muthoot Finance did not reply to a Reuters email seeking comment.

data.title: Investors Continue to Shift Assets Away from U.S., Hurting Dollar — Market Talk
data.description: RBC BlueBay Asset Management continues to hear of asset allocation shifts which are taking demand away from the U.S. dollar, says fixed income CIO Mark Dowding in a note. "This trend can continue," he says. If there is a renewed period of volatility, the dollar could weaken more rapidly. This could be triggered by fears that the dollar has tended to trade like a risk asset over recent months, he says. The DXY dollar index--which measures the dollar's value against a basket of currencies--rises 0.3% at 98.997. It hit a six-week low of 98.351 on Thursday. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Market Chatter: Delta Air Lines Sold $2 Billion of Bonds to Repay Loan Taken to Pay Workers During Pandemic
data.description:
Delta Air Lines sold $2 billion of the three-year and five-year investment-grade bonds to repay a loan it took from the US government in 2020, Bloomberg News reported Thursday, citing an unnamed person with knowledge of the matter.

The $1.6 billion loan due in 2030 was taken under a program established by the CARES Act, which allowed airlines struggling during the pandemic to borrow money to pay their employees, according to the report.

This was the largest of the three loans Delta received through the Payroll Support Program, Bloomberg said, adding that the remaining loans will mature a year later than the one being refinanced.

Delta did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment on the matter.

Australia Could Ease U.S. Beef Import Rules Amid Tariff Tensions
data.description:
By Stuart Condie

SYDNEY--Australia could ease rules on U.S. beef imports amid tensions over U.S. tariffs including on steel and aluminum.

Australia allows the import of beef from cattle raised and slaughtered in the U.S. However, beef from U.S.-slaughtered cattle raised in Mexico and Canada isn't allowed into a country with strict biosecurity rules designed to protect local industry and animals.

Australia's Prime Minister Anthony Albanese on Friday said his government could consider modifying the rules. White House officials called out Australia's policy on beef imports when justifying the imposition of tariffs earlier this year.

"If things can be sorted out in a way that protects our biosecurity, of course we don't just say 'no,'" Albanese said in a radio interview.

"We don't want imports in here for the sake of it. Our first priority is biosecurity and there'll be no compromise on that."

As a major producer, Australia hasn't imported any U.S. beef since 2005. Beef was Australia's seventh-largest export by value in its last full fiscal year, behind mined commodities including iron ore and travel-related services.

data.title: Market Chatter: McDonald's Maintains Core Inclusion Programs Despite Backlash
data.description:
McDonald's is maintaining its core inclusion programs despite backlash towards its diversity, equity and inclusion, or DEI, initiatives, Bloomberg News reported, citing McDonald's USA Chief Field People Officer Jordann Nunn.

Earlier this year, the fast-food giant retired "aspirational representation goals" for its workforce and a diversity pledge for its supply chain. It also adopted the "inclusion" terminology instead of DEI, though it has kept several other programs intact, according to the report.

"We changed some of the language that we've used it around it, but at the core none of our programming has changed," Bloomberg reported, citing Nunn. "We have no intention of doing that."

McDonald's didn't immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment.

data.title: EU open to lowering tariffs on US fertilisers in trade talks
data.description:
The European Union is open to lowering tariffs on U.S. fertiliser imports as an offer in trade talks with the Trump administration, but will not weaken its food safety standards in pursuit of a deal, EU agriculture commissioner Christophe Hansen told Reuters.

"That is definitely an option," Hansen said, of reducing U.S. fertiliser tariffs.

"That will be on the table. And I think that would be a huge way forward, and an offer as well to the U.S.," he said in an interview with Reuters on Thursday, adding that whether that would mean zero tariffs, or a reduction of current rates, would need to be negotiated.

U.S. exports face the EU's standard tariffs of 5.5% on imports of ammonia, and 6.5% on nitrogen fertilisers, as well as an extra 29.48 euro-per-tonne anti-dumping duty on U.S. urea ammonium nitrate (UAN).

UAN comprised around three quarters of EU imports of U.S. fertilisers last year, EU trade data shows.

Reducing tariffs could boost Europe's purchases of U.S. fertiliser, to fill a gap as the EU cuts supplies from Russia. Around 24% of the EU's nitrogen fertiliser imports came from Russia in 2023, while the U.S. accounted for 8%, EU data shows.

"I believe most of the Europeans would prefer buying fertilizers from the U.S. than from Russia," Hansen said.

The EU will hit nitrogen-based fertilisers from Russia with tariffs rising to 100% over three years, a level that would effectively halt annual trade flows currently worth 1.3 billion euros ($1.5 billion).

Hansen said the EU was also open to discussing increasing its purchases of hormone-free beef from the U.S., and a deal to have zero-for-zero tariffs on EU and U.S. wines.

But he said the bloc would not compromise on its stringent food safety standards as it seeks a deal.

"I don't see room for manoeuvre to roll back our high quality standards. But of course, on other points, on other products, we are very open to negotiations," Hansen said.

data.title: Polyvinyl chloride (PVC) export prices quote higher in the United States
data.description: This week, PVC export prices gained in the US.An industry source in the U.S. informed a Polymerupdate team member, " The US PVC market experienced a significant increase in activity throughout the week, bolstered by strong demand from major export regions like Latin America and Africa, alongside supply constraints. Multiple producers either held back on making spot offers or functioned with restricted availability, limiting buyer choices in the export market. Consequently, a few suppliers took advantage of the scarcity to request price hikes for June deliveries. Initially, consumers in specific regions opposed the rising price changes, mentioning worries about affordability and unpredictable future demand. Nonetheless, as supply limitations continued and accessible quantities started to decrease, market resistance slowly eased. By the week's end, at least one key producer had exhausted its available supplies, indicating that purchasers had started to accept the elevated prices�either due to necessity or to obtain materials before anticipated further increases. This change signifies increasing momentum in the US PVC export market, as tighter fundamentals bolster stronger pricing sentiment leading into June. FAS Houston PVC export prices were assessed at USD 650-660/mt, a rise of USD (+15/mt) from the previous week.Spot feedstock FOB US Gulf export EDC prices were assessed at the USD 95-105/mt levels, a spike of USD (+20/mt) from last week. Meanwhile, VCM prices on Friday were assessed flat at the USD 465-475/mt FOB US Gulf levels.Spot upstream ethylene prices on Thursday were assessed at 19.00-19.50 cents/lb FOB U.S. Gulf, a week on week decline of (-0.50 cents/lb).In Asia, PVC prices were assessed at USD 690-7200/mt CFR China, stable from the previous week.

data.title: Meheco Subsidiary Gets China Nod for Anti-Blood Clotting Drug
data.description:
China's medical products administrator granted China Meheco subsidiary Tianfang Pharmaceutical a drug registration certificate for its clopidogrel bisulfate tablets for blood clotting prevention.

The drug is mainly used for the secondary prevention of atherosclerotic thrombotic events in patients with heart attack, stroke, or other coronary conditions, according to a Friday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

data.title: Clock is ticking down to US non-farm payrolls
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 flat
  • Eyes on payrolls
  • Wall St futures rise

CLOCK TICKS DOWN TO US NON-FARM PAYROLLS

U.S. non farm payrolls slipped your mind? With other more gripping drama unfolding, it can be forgiven.

But market watchers will have to tear their eyes away in a few hours; the print remains vital for a steer on the outlook for Fed policy.

A wide range of forecasts for May reflects the foggy economy outlook. According to LSEG data, expectations for the increase in non-farm payrolls ranges from 75k-190k.

A Reuters poll puts consensus expectations at 130k which would be a marked decrease from April's 177k. The unemployment rate is expected to stay at 4.2%.

BofA expects labour market resilience, with an above consensus 150k increase.

"This should keep the Fed on hold for an extended period, against a more constructive rates view that the market focusing more on 'stag' than 'flation' seems to have."

UBS' chief economist Paul Donovan notes though that most forecasts are ringing in below consensus.

"Firms may have slowed hiring as trade policy uncertainty has increased, but there is unlikely to be an increase in firing. That does mean that rate cuts would have a limited impact right now (although if consumer demand weakens, rate cuts become more important)," he said.

Citi sees an increase of 120k, while BNP Paribas comes in with 125k.

"We could see a number closer to 50k than 150k, however," warns Neil Wilson, UK investor strategist at Saxo Markets.

Indeed, the ADP National Employment Report released on Wednesday came in well below expectations with a meager 37k private payrolls added. Consensus had been for a 110k rise.

Markets are fully backing no change from the Fed at its June meeting in a few weeks.

But a weak non-farm payrolls print today may bolster bets for a cut.

(Lucy Raitano)

data.title: McEwen Mining Breaks Ground at Fox Complex's Stock Mine
data.description:
McEwen Mining overnight Thursday said it broke ground at its Fox Complex's Stock Mine, located along the Destor-Porcupine fault, with construction of the ramp system now underway to provide access to future lower-cost-per-ounce gold production.

"As we transition production from the Froome Mine to the Stock Mine, the timing is ideal for bringing Stock Mine into production," said McEwen Mining Chief Executive Rob McEwen.

"Moreover, it is important to understand that the Stock Mine will help us increase our near-term gold production and decrease our cost per ounce for three reasons: One, it will eliminate the onerous metal stream from prior owners on the Froome mine, which obligated us to sell 8% of our production at $605 per ounce; Two, the Stock material is softer than that currently processed from Froome, which will enable greater mill throughput and gold output; and Three, with the mill on site, we eliminate the cost of hauling material 35 kilometers from Froome," continued McEwen.

U.S.-listed shares of the company fell nearly 2% to $9.10 in pre-market trading.

data.title: British Recycler Renewi's CEO Steps Down
data.description:
Renewi said Friday that Chief Executive Otto de Bont stepped down.

The British pure-play recycling company will announce a successor in due course.
